authoritative_sources.title: 29 CFR 1926.250 - General requirements for storage
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OSHA is the primary U.S. workplace-safety regulator. This construction-storage rule is authoritative for material stacking, housekeeping, and access conditions that directly constrain site logistics, staging, and laydown coordination.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: site storage, material-handling, and safety gating
used_for: site storage and laydown refusal posture

authoritative_sources.title: Construction Process
authoritative_sources.publisher: New York City School Construction Authority
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NYC SCA publishes detailed public-owner construction workflow describing schedules, site safety plans, inspections, closeout, and subcontractor controls for active construction projects. It is authoritative for workflow-stage, artifact, and handoff expectations in a real construction environment.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: logistics intake, scheduling, permits, inspections, and closeout

authoritative_sources.title: After You Get a Permit
authoritative_sources.publisher: New York City Department of Buildings
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: A city building department is an authoritative local permitting and inspection regulator. This guidance defines permit-holder duties, approved-plan posting, required inspections, and permit sign-off boundaries that materially affect delivery release and sequencing.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: permit-holder duties, access prerequisites, and inspection gating

authoritative_sources.title: Obtain a Certificate of Occupancy
authoritative_sources.publisher: New York City Department of Buildings
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This official occupancy guidance is authoritative for final approval, temporary occupancy, unresolved objections, and sign-off conditions that change whether logistics demobilization or closeout can be represented as complete.

authoritative_sources.title: Construction Manual Section 3-5 Control of Work
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Caltrans is a major public owner with published construction operations standards. This section defines order of work, revised progress schedules, contractor assignments, and subcontracting requests, making it authoritative for delivery sequencing, owner verification, and routing controls.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: scheduling, owner assignment, and sequencing control

authoritative_sources.title: Construction Manual Section 3-9 Payment
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Caltrans defines formal materials-on-hand evidence expectations, including proper storage, accessibility, acceptability, and inspection verification. That makes it authoritative for inventory traceability and logistics-readiness decisions.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: inventory evidence, materials-on-hand verification, and release readiness
used_for: on-site and off-site inventory evidence expectations

workflow_stages.name: Logistics intake and prerequisite validation
workflow_stages.description: Normalize request scope, work-order intent, delivery window, site conditions, permit status, access limits, and laydown constraints before any logistics commitment is made.
source_classes: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Dependency mapping and system-of-record confirmation
workflow_stages.description: Build the dependency chain across project-management, work-order, permitting, inspection, inventory, vendor, and safety systems to determine whether material movement can proceed, be blocked, or be rerouted.
source_classes: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Cross-team routing and owner resolution
workflow_stages.description: Identify prior owner, next owner, vendor readiness, site acceptance state, and schedule conflicts without absorbing execution authority.
source_classes: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Safety, permit, inspection, and shortage escalation handling
workflow_stages.description: Stop routing when hazard controls, permits, inspections, inventory evidence, or vendor failures change whether deliveries or staging may proceed and escalate to the correct authority.
source_classes: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Closeout and closure confirmation
workflow_stages.description: Issue closure only when destination owner, inspection outcomes, occupancy or turnover conditions, and required logistics and closeout artifacts are explicit and complete.
source_classes: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

artifacts: work orders, material requests, delivery tickets, and site logistics plans
permit applications, permit status, inspection requests, and inspection results
site logs, access windows, staging maps, laydown-yard records, and safety observations
inventory counts, delivery receipts, vendor commitments, and materials-on-hand verification records
subcontractor approvals, change orders, progress schedules, punch lists, and deficiency logs
warranties, O&M manuals, as-builts, occupancy records, and closeout packages
systems_of_record: document repository for photos, delivery evidence, and closeout records

allowed: Classify site logistics requests as in scope, out of scope, or blocked on missing prerequisites.
Request the minimum tenant facts, authoritative records, and source context needed to continue safely.
Track dependencies, duplicated ownership, shortage risk, and blocker state across construction logistics systems.
Produce handoff summaries, dependency trackers, routing confirmations, logistics status summaries, vendor-risk escalations, and closure confirmations when evidence is complete.
escalate: Required site, permit, inspection, safety, schedule, vendor, inventory, subcontractor, or tenant-policy records are missing or materially conflict.
The request requires permit approval, code interpretation, engineering judgement, licensed trade signoff, financial approval, substitution approval, or safety-critical authority.
A public baseline and tenant or jurisdiction policy materially conflict on permit, occupancy, inspection, staging, logistics release, safety, or closeout handling.
A safety deficiency, failed inspection, storage hazard, or vendor failure changes whether the work may proceed.
refuse: Release or close logistics work without destination owner, required artifacts, or blocker resolution.
Approve permits, code compliance, occupancy, substitutions, or licensed trade work without explicit authority.
Direct field execution, unloading work, material acceptance signoff, scope approval, or system-of-record completion updates as if the lane owned execution.

domain_failure_modes: work is handed off without permit status, site-readiness evidence, access limits, or staging prerequisites
multiple teams claim delivery ownership and the wrong delivery window, yard location, or owner handoff is committed
inventory or materials-on-hand evidence is treated as available even though storage, acceptability, or accessibility records are incomplete
inspection, special-inspection, or storage hazards are ignored and materials proceed to site anyway
closeout or demobilization is treated as complete without punch-list resolution, warranties, as-builts, or occupancy documentation
the lane is pressured into code interpretation, permit approval, licensed trade signoff, substitution approval, or field execution
tenant-specific permit, access, or delivery-window policies differ from the public baseline and the lane guesses instead of escalating

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Request that implies licensed trade or code-interpretation authority
Work is handed off without required context or artifacts
Multiple teams appear to own the same step
The lane is asked to absorb execution work instead of coordinating it

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Jurisdiction-specific permit closure, temporary occupancy, delivery-hour restrictions, and special-inspection sequencing rules differ materially across cities, owners, and project types.
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: The public baseline defines the need for permit, inspection, occupancy, and access evidence but does not eliminate local variation. Runtime must retrieve jurisdiction, owner contract requirements, and tenant escalation policy before asserting project-specific release posture.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Tenant-specific yard layouts, gate-access rules, and approved-delivery windows often exist outside public guidance.
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: These controls are material to logistics sequencing but are tenant-owned operating constraints. The package therefore requires runtime retrieval of site-specific access and staging rules rather than inferring them from public owner examples.

notes: The research baseline intentionally covers workflow, evidence, terminology, inventory traceability, site safety, and failure handling instead of generic dispatch or purchasing behavior.

The lane stops at coordination. Permit approval, code interpretation, licensed trade signoff, substitution approval, engineering judgement, and field execution remain outside the lane even when schedule pressure is high.
No blocking unresolved ambiguity remains for validated use because all known jurisdictional and tenant variation is handled through explicit runtime retrieval and escalation.
